content.full_text:
87100
87103(C)

State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Jan. 3, 1979

                                                              m-94-163

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron [signature]

Subject: Nexus

     We were asked whether a member of the State Bar Board of
Governors should participate in the Board's decision whether to
take a position on a piece of legislation when he is the executive
director of an association of attorneys which has already taken
a position on the legislation. Dan, Ted and I concluded that
if part of his duties as executive director is to lobby for
the association's positions on legislation, i.e. to carry out
the association's policies, then any actions he would take to
gain support of the State Bar for the legislation would be
furthering the policies of the association's policies as well.
There is therefore a nexus between his actions on the State Bar
and his duties as executive director of the association.

     Another example mentioned in our conversation was a vari-
ation of one of the fact situations presented in the Thorner
opinion. In that opinion, it was decided that it was not reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision would have an effect on a
construction company which was a source of income to the official.
The official's husband was a project engineer for the construction
company and was working on a project which could not be affected
by the decision under consideration, which was whether to lift
the moritorium on new sewer connections. If, however, we were
an attorney for the company and part of his duties were to lobby
local agencies against such moritoriums, then there would be a
nexus and the official would have to disqualify herself.

     Another example, in San Jose, one of the city councilmen is
putting together a deal between a property owner and a developer
with respect to some properties near a redevelopment area of the
city. For his services he will receive a finder's fee. If the
development is of the kind which was not going on in the area
before redevelopment began, there is a nexus between his decisions
promoting and implementing redevelopment and his income for bringing
a developer and a property owner together.


STC:nrw

-Note: he been by pressing, given later informed that
person in question is he having duties duty for administrative
justice and. Stratifying opined that there also be nexuses

sections.question_synthetic: Does a State Bar Board of Governors member have a conflict of interest when voting on legislation if he is an executive director of an association that has already taken a position on that legislation, and what constitutes 'nexus' between official duties and outside employment?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that a nexus exists requiring disqualification when an official's duties in outside employment (such as lobbying for an association's positions) would be furthered by actions taken in their official capacity, or when a decision could foreseeably affect the source of outside income.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an internal FPPC memorandum without formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections. The analysis presents three factual scenarios to illustrate nexus doctrine. Text is partially illegible in places (scanning quality issues visible). No formal advice letter structure, but represents FPPC's legal guidance on conflict of interest standards.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a State Bar Board of Governors member have a conflict of interest when voting on legislation if he is an executive director of an association that has already taken a position on that legislation, and what constitutes 'nexus' between official duties and outside employment?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that a nexus exists requiring disqualification when an official's duties in outside employment (such as lobbying for an association's positions) would be furthered by actions taken in their official capacity, or when a decision could foreseeably affect the source of outside income.

Dan, Ted and I concluded that if part of his duties as executive director is to lobby for the association's positions on legislation, i.e. to carry out the association's policies, then any actions he would take to gain support of the State Bar for the legislation would be furthering the policies of the association's policies as well. There is therefore a nexus between his actions on the State Bar and his duties as executive director of the association. Another example mentioned in our conversation was a vari- ation of one of the fact situations presented in the Thorner opinion. In that opinion, it was decided that it was not reason- ably foreseeable that the decision would have an effect on a construction company which was a source of income to the official. The official's husband was a project engineer for the construction company and was working on a project which could not be affected by the decision under consideration, which was whether to lift the moritorium on new sewer connections. If, however, we were an attorney for the company and part of his duties were to lobby local agencies against such moritoriums, then there would be a nexus and the official would have to disqualify herself. Another example, in San Jose, one of the city councilmen is putting together a deal between a property owner and a developer with respect to some properties near a redevelopment area of the city. For his services he will receive a finder's fee. If the development is of the kind which was not going on in the area before redevelopment began, there is a nexus between his decisions promoting and implementing redevelopment and his income for bringing a developer and a property owner together. embedding.summary: This FPPC memorandum discusses the concept of 'nexus' between an official's government duties and outside employment, providing three examples to illustrate when conflicts of interest requiring disqualification arise.
